topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white walls of Dr. Albright’s office felt like a personal affront. I stood by the door, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame, the sound of my own ragged breathing loud in the quiet room. He sat behind the mahogany desk, radiating an unnerving calm, his posture impeccably straight, the very picture of professional composure. It was him. Mark. The boy who used to trip me in the hallway, whose cruel laughter used to echo in my ears, was now diagnosing my deepest anxieties. A slow, hot pressure built behind my eyes. I took a deliberate step forward, the floorboards groaning under my weight, and the air crackled with unspoken fury. "You think you can just waltz in here and pretend you understand anything?" My voice was dangerously low, a brittle sound that barely masked the tremor running through my hands. He merely tilted his head, a faint, knowing smile playing on his lips, completely unperturbed by the storm brewing in front of him.

[story 2]

I watched him from across the therapy couch, every muscle in my jaw tight enough to crack stone. The casual way he adjusted his glasses, the practiced empathy in his tone—it was all a performance, a masterful deception. I felt a sudden, violent need to shatter something, anything, to break through the veneer of his practiced gentleness. My fists clenched so tightly my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. "Don't patronize me," I spat out, the words sharp and laced with venom. I wanted to shove him, to see the composure finally crack, to witness the raw, ugly reaction beneath the therapist’s polished exterior. He simply leaned forward, his eyes locking onto mine, and in that moment, I saw not the tormentor, but something chillingly competent. The heat rising in my chest was almost unbearable, a suffocating pressure demanding release.

[story 3]

The silence stretched, thick and suffocating, punctuated only by the faint hum of the air conditioning. I paced the perimeter of the room, my movements jerky and sharp, like a caged animal fighting for space. Every glance I threw his way was laced with accusation, a silent scream demanding accountability. I could feel the blood pounding a frantic rhythm against my temples. This wasn't about healing; this was about desecration. "You haven't changed a damn thing," I finally managed, the words coming out strained and ragged, a desperate attempt to keep my voice steady while the internal pressure threatened to explode. He watched my agitation with an almost detached curiosity, a slight narrowing of his eyes that somehow felt worse than any direct insult. It was a cold, surgical assessment, and it ignited a furious, burning resentment deep within my core.
